summary_en: An algorithm that corrects node labels until they give shortest distances from an origin.
A path-building module in the HUD transit battery.

Class of random utility models introduced by McFadden (1981) in which indirect utility is linear in income, allowing Marshallian and Hicksian demand to coincide and social aggregation of preferences for welfare analysis.
Problem of generating aggregate travel demand information by summing individual choice-model contributions over population, level-of-service and spatial variation.
Survey information on attitudes or perceptions used in early modal choice investigations to represent attributes such as comfort, convenience or reliability.
Parameter in Tanner's logistic car ownership model representing the upper limit of vehicle ownership per person approached over time.
Function whose gradient gives choice probabilities consistent with random utility maximisation, used by Fosgerau et al (2013) to characterise additive RUM models.
Average or aggregate measure over modal or route costs used by Wilson (1967, 1970) to link sequential travel forecasting stages within the entropy maximising framework.
A planning process covering land use and future demand for public and private passenger and goods modes.
Economic measure of user benefit, used in transportation project evaluation as a change in surplus relative to the do-nothing or reference situation.
Parameter λ in entropy maximising and multinomial logit models that governs the dispersion of travel patterns and the sensitivity of demand to changes in generalised cost.
Measure of responsiveness of demand or choice to changes in explanatory variables, used in the source to assess the response properties of travel forecasting models.
Expected value of the distribution of maximum utility over a choice set, shown by McFadden (1978) to equal (up to a constant) the logarithm of the GEV generating function G.
The return of results from later forecasting stages to revise earlier assumptions and repeat the procedure.
An empirical travel-time factor representing the effect of spatial separation on trip interchange.

Linear combination of travel attributes (typically in-vehicle time, excess time and money cost) weighted by behavioural values of time, used in UK travel forecasting and evaluation from the late 1960s.
Criterion for assessing how well a model reproduces observed data, described in the source as insufficient on its own for validating travel forecasting models.

canonical_name: Gumbel Type I Extreme Value Distribution

names.text: Gumbel Type I Extreme Value Distribution

summary_en: Extreme-value distribution used in the source's derivation of the MNL model from IID random utility residuals.
User taxes identified as a funding source for interstate highway construction.
Assumption that random utility or error terms for alternatives are identically and independently distributed.
Information-transfer quantity linking levels of a decision hierarchy in early discrete-choice demand models.
Choice axiom requiring odds between two alternatives to be independent of other alternatives in the choice set.

canonical_name: Interzonal Modal Travel Time and Cost

names.text: Interzonal Modal Travel Time and Cost

summary_en: Zone-pair mode-specific travel times and costs used in modal choice.
A zone-to-zone adjustment factor in the BPR gravity model.

canonical_name: Land Use Forecasting in the UK

names.text: Land Use Forecasting in the UK

summary_en: Approach to forecasting future land-use distribution and characteristics in UK transportation studies, described by Bruton (1975) as largely ad hoc and crude compared with US practice.
The class of urban land use models criticized by Lee.
Variable that cannot be measured directly but is inferred from relationships with observable variables, used in hybrid choice models to represent attitudes and perceptions.
Form of the mixed multinomial logit model in which the random component has a general covariance structure, combining probit-like disturbances with independent Gumbel variation.
Distribution of random parameters in the random parameters logit form of the mixed multinomial logit model.
Ben-Akiva's group of infrequently made choices such as employment and residential location, housing type, car ownership and work mode choice.
Term used by Williams and Senior (1977) for the empirical violation of the nested logit parameter inequality condition λ_D ≤ λ_M in widely adopted G/D/M/A model structures.
Harris and Tanner case in which utility is written as a common term plus a random error independent of the representative value.
The correspondence between forecast and observed travel outcomes, assessed by Mackinder and Evans (1981) through ex post comparison with out-turn data for UK studies up to 1971.
Transportation illustration of IIA failure when one bus alternative is artificially partitioned into red and blue bus alternatives.
Attribute of a scheduling choice introduced by Small (1982), measuring the deviation between actual arrival time and a preferred or official arrival time.
The network problem of finding minimum-cost routes from an origin.
Problem in MNL applications where alternatives are close substitutes and the IIA property yields implausible substitution patterns.
Evaluation framework used to assess whether transportation projects are worthwhile by comparing social costs and benefits, typically incorporating user benefit measures such as the Rule-of-a-Half.
Class of models describing flows between origins and destinations, often derived from entropy maximisation or constrained optimisation and later reinterpreted within a random utility framework.
The housing-market process emulated by the Herbert–Stevens model.
Psychological utility concept used in Luce's axiomatic derivation of probabilistic choice models.
Accessibility by car and public transport used in urban location models.
Early-1970s transportation planning emphasis contrasted with long-range highway-dominated planning in the source chapter.
A resistance term associated with separation or route conditions between zones.
Ben-Akiva's group of frequent non-work trip choices such as frequency, destination, mode, time of day and route.

Term used by Marvin Manheim (1973) for the conventional four-stage urban travel forecasting model system, which by the early 1970s was being criticised for policy insensitivity, spatial aggregation, and limited behavioural response.
An integrated metropolitan study combining travel surveys, forecasts, assignment and transportation planning.
Willingness to pay for reductions in travel time variability, expressed as a monetary value of journey time reliability.
Trade-off between travel time and money cost derived from disaggregate modal choice studies, used to weight components of generalised cost.
Monetary value that an individual is prepared to give up to obtain a change in an attribute, frequently derived from discrete choice models.
A zonal total or average used as an explanatory variable in early trip generation models.

Oil crisis of late 1973 that triggered economic austerity and a shift in transportation planning priorities away from large-scale highway capital projects toward public transport, systems management and restraint measures.
1972 conference described by Boyce and Williams as giving impetus to an individual motivation and choice approach to travel forecasting.
A deterministic loading method that assigns each O-D flow to a shortest route.
Traffic assignment applied across a metropolitan-area network rather than a single corridor.
UK stochastic route choice method for highway and public transport assignment using rectangular probability distributions of link times, developed by Burrell (1969).
A traffic assignment method that modifies link travel conditions to account for congestion.
Approximation for the distribution of the maximum of two jointly normal variables, applied by Daganzo et al (1977) to simplify multinomial probit numerical integration.

Marketing and psychology technique, originating in the 1960s and 1970s, in which individuals value attributes constituting alternatives; applied in transportation as a precursor to stated preference methods.
A method for mapping densities of straight-line trip paths across an area.
Micro-level travel-demand modelling approach based on individual or household data and behavioural choice hypotheses.
Statistical model of individual travellers' mode choice, pioneered by Stanley Warner in his 1962 Chicago study.
Multivariate statistical technique used by Warner and Quarmby to allocate individuals to modes in modal choice studies.

Approach to constructing stated preference experiments that seeks efficiency or optimality for estimating the parameters of non-linear discrete choice models, as an alternative to traditional orthogonal designs.

Statistical-mechanics-derived method introduced by Alan Wilson (1967) for deriving spatial interaction, trip distribution and modal split models as the most likely trip pattern consistent with observed constraints.
Neuberger's Method I, which evaluates user benefits by multiplying the fixed trip matrix by changes in user costs, ignoring demand responses other than route switching.
A growth-factor method for adjusting an observed trip table to forecast trip-end totals.

Sequential four-stage travel forecasting structure in which modal split follows trip distribution, adopted in the SELNEC Transportation Study and increasingly common in UK studies after the mid-1960s.

Five necessary and sufficient conditions derived by Zachary (1976) for a probabilistic choice model to be derivable from translational invariant utility functions, used by Daly and Zachary (1978) to validate the nested logit model.
Model structure arranging conditional MNL or logit models in a hierarchy over multiple travel choice dimensions.
A household interview survey recording residents, vehicles and 24-hour trips.

Neuberger's Method III, a generalisation of the Marshallian consumer surplus measure using the path integral of demand with respect to generalised cost, requiring integrability conditions satisfied by multinomial logit demand models.
UK trip generation method in which zonal productions are built from household categories with stable trip rates, introduced by Wootton and Pick in the mid-1960s.
Framework that integrates discrete choice methods with latent variable models, allowing explicit modelling of latent psychological constructs, heterogeneity and latent segmentation.
A method for generating and evaluating internally consistent land use and transportation alternatives.

canonical_name: Integrated Trip Distribution and Traffic Assignment Procedure

names.text: Integrated Trip Distribution and Traffic Assignment Procedure

summary_en: A CATS procedure coupling trip distribution with network assignment.
A sequence of all-or-nothing assignments with updated link speeds.
Method used in the SYNSAM procedure to construct and update contingency tables for household characteristics from marginal tabulations.
The iterative procedure used to solve interdependent Lowry Model allocations.
Statistical model in which categorical latent variables identify population segments with similar taste parameters, choice sets or decision mechanisms.
Public transport network representation using fixed routes, stops, headways and interchanges, pioneered in the UK by Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith and Associates in the mid-1960s.
The optimization framework used to formulate the Herbert–Stevens model.
Aggregation method dividing the population into homogeneous groups and substituting average independent-variable values for each segment.
Estimation approach proposed by Bhat (2011) for multinomial probit models, reported to be more computationally efficient than simulation-based maximum simulated likelihood.
Estimation method used for parameters of linear-utility MNL models in early disaggregate travel demand applications.
Estimation approach in which simulated choice frequencies replace analytical probabilities in the likelihood function, introduced by Lerman and Manski (1981).
Simulation-based estimator derived from the generalised method of moments, developed by McFadden (1989) and McFadden and Ruud (1994) to avoid multiple integrals in discrete choice models.
Iterative traffic assignment technique in which successive link volume estimates receive equal weight, used by the SELNEC Transportation Study.
Numerical technique discussed in connection with solving individual choice models in the discrete-choice random utility framework.
Method introduced in the Dutch transferability study in which scaling factors multiply parts of the generalised cost function to transfer disaggregate choice models from one area to another.
The origin-and-destination factoring method used by DMATS to forecast zonal interchanges.
Numerical method using random numbers to solve integration problems, widely applied in microsimulation and discrete choice model estimation.
Choice model form used by Wilson (1969, 1970) to express trip distribution, modal split and route split as shares of a travel market, derivable from entropy maximisation.
Early aggregation procedure substituting mean explanatory-variable values into individual choice probabilities for a group.

Method proposed by Bradley and Daly (1991) for accommodating differential variance between revealed and stated preference data within a nested logit model by creating an artificial tree with SP and RP alternatives.
A survey-based method for documenting movements between origins and destinations.
Deterministic number sequence approach used for numerical integration and simulation in the estimation of mixed logit and other open-form discrete choice models.
Discrete-choice model structure expressing joint choices as marginal and conditional probability models ordered in a hierarchy.

Computerised model application procedure developed in the San Francisco Bay Area MTC study, compatible with traditional matrix manipulation and assignment procedures, using zonal data and market segmentation.

Standard approach in the 1970s for estimating discrete choice random utility models from observed choices and socio-economic data, with limitations such as scarce information on infrequently chosen alternatives and multicollinearity among attributes.

names.text: variance scaling of RP and SP data

summary_en: Method proposed by Morikawa (1989) for adjusting the variance of unobserved effects in stated preference data so that pooled revealed and stated preference discrete choice models have equal variances.

Ad hoc marginal approximation to the change in Marshallian consumer surplus, calculated as the change in generalised cost for each O-D pair and mode multiplied by the average number of trips in the with and without schemes; became standard practice in UK scheme evaluation.
Aggregation method applying a disaggregate model separately to sample members and expanding the sample to represent the population.
A staged procedure linking inventories, land-use and activity forecasts, travel estimates, network loading and plan refinement.

Computerised model application procedure developed in the San Francisco Bay Area MTC study to produce information on broadly defined transportation policy options over short and medium time frames, based on random-sample forecasting.
Discrete-choice model structure treating multiple choice dimensions on the same footing, such as destination and mode combinations in an MNL model.
The equations used by EMPIRIC to allocate population and employment changes.
Stated preference approach in which individuals choose between hypothetical combinations of attributes, allowing direct estimation of discrete choice models and prediction of market shares.
Experimental methods for examining attitudes, preferences and choices as a basis for travel forecasting.
Robert Dial's stochastic network loading method for distributing O-D flows over multiple routes.

Method of creating representative samples of individuals or households from secondary data sources or by augmenting existing samples, used for aggregation and forecasting with disaggregate choice models.
Traditional aggregate urban travel forecasting procedure criticised for theoretical, statistical, behavioural and policy-sensitivity limitations.

canonical_name: Travel Choice Analysis with Random Cost Distributions

names.text: Travel Choice Analysis with Random Cost Distributions
analysis of a travel choice situation with random distributions of costs

summary_en: Travel-choice analysis framed with random distributions of costs, attributed in the source to Claude Abraham's late-1950s and 1961 work.
A CATS assignment procedure that loaded each shortest-route tree after updating link times.
Label used for practical disaggregate travel forecasting efforts applying behaviourally sophisticated models as alternatives to conventional methods.
The travel forecasting stage that allocates trips between origin and destination zones.
Methods for forecasting trip productions and attractions from zonal or household characteristics.
An iterative adjustment of interchange volumes toward predicted terminal totals.
Forecasting practice for urban travel demand and behaviour, including aggregate and disaggregate modelling approaches discussed in the source.
Early trip generation method based on regression equations fitted to zonal totals, later displaced by household-based category analysis for trip production in larger UK studies.
The capacity term in the CATS congested travel-time function.
A historical capacity measure defined by acceptable delay, hazard and freedom to maneuver.
An aggregated activity-location model that replaced the RGM in the PJTS program.
The empirical gravity-model implementation documented for BPR computer programs.
A link performance function relating assigned volume and practical capacity to travel time.
The CATS exponential link travel-time function based on volume and design capacity.
Passenger demand model for the Channel Tunnel study described in a note as hierarchical logit with generalised cost in power-function form.
McFadden's econometric choice model developed in detail as a useful empirical case in his random-utility framework.

Model proposed by Williams (1977a) for destination–mode choice combinations with more complex patterns of similarity than nested logit structures; not consistent with utility maximisation in its original form.
Member of the generalised extreme value family that allows alternatives to belong to more than one nest, capturing cross-similarities among pure and combined modes.
Probabilistic model family for travel choices among alternatives, developed within an economic framework and often based on random utility maximisation.
A fitted relationship between zonal trip interchanges, trip ends and zone separation.
Entropy maximising journey-to-work trip distribution model constrained by origin productions and destination attractions, introduced by Wilson (1967).
Tversky model mentioned by McFadden as a possible source of practical functional forms without IIA.
A regression-based urban development model developed for the Boston Regional Planning Project.
McFadden model class discussed by Boyce and Williams as containing the nested logit model as an important special case.
A spatial interaction model used to distribute trips or truck flows.

canonical_name: Helvig Gravity Model of External Truck Movements

names.text: Helvig Gravity Model of External Truck Movements

summary_en: A gravity model applied to truck movements entering the Chicago study area.
A linear-programming residential location model designed to emulate spatial market clearing.
Discrete choice model formulated by Bhat (1995) in which Gumbel error terms are allowed to have different variances across alternatives, requiring a one-dimensional integration.

Special case of the elimination-by-aspects model proposed by Tversky and Sattath (1979) in which subsets of alternatives sharing aspects form a preference tree, structurally similar to the nested logit (tree extreme value) model.
Nested or hierarchical probit freight model for route and mode combinations in the Channel Tunnel study.
Hierarchical model treating public transport as a composite mode before dividing demand among public transport submodes.
A gravity-based model for interzonal truck flows in Toronto.
Modal split calibration and application modules in the HUD transit battery.
Microsimulation housing market model developed by Kain and Apgar (1985) at Harvard in the early 1980s.
A trip distribution model based on ordered destination opportunities and a probability of acceptance.
RUM model class in which alternative utilities are represented as systematic utility plus random residuals independent of systematic utility.

canonical_name: Joint Car Ownership-Work Mode Choice Model

names.text: Joint Car Ownership-Work Mode Choice Model

summary_en: Joint disaggregate MNL model for household car ownership and primary worker work mode choice.
Simultaneous MNL structure over destination and mode combinations for shopping trips in Ben-Akiva's study.
Multi-modal, multi-person-type model of multinomial logit form introduced by Wilson (1967) that jointly determines destination and mode choice.
A two-sector urban activity location model also called Model of Metropolis.

names.text: Micro-Analytical Simulation of Transportation, Employment and Residence

summary_en: Urban microsimulation model developed by Roger Mackett (1985) capturing interdependencies between transportation, employment and residence.
Open-form discrete choice random utility model in which random parameters or error components allow for taste heterogeneity and flexible correlation patterns among alternatives.
Discrete choice random utility model in which the random error terms are independent and identically Gumbel distributed, giving a closed-form choice probability expression.
General choice model discussed in the source as a possible mid-1970s route for addressing similarity issues in multimodal choice.
Discrete choice model based on normal random utility distributions, described as computationally onerous for multiple alternatives.
Discrete-choice random utility model presented as a practical generalisation of MNL that avoids the IIA property.
GEV model formulation by Daly and Bierlaire, equivalent to the recursive nested extreme value model, using a network structure to capture correlation among alternatives.
Choice model class in which selection probabilities are derived from utility, psychological choice assumptions, or distributions of utilities.

Land use forecasting model adopted in the San Francisco Bay Area MTC study to project future land use, employment location, residential location, housing-type choices and socio-economic characteristics.
Choice model class attributing random utility variation to distributions of taste parameters in representative utility or generalised cost.
Form of the mixed multinomial logit model in which the parameters of the representative utility vary over the population according to a mixing distribution.
Generalisation of the cross-nested logit model proposed by Andrew Daly, based on multiple layering of nests.
The PJTS model proposed to simulate location, trip making, land markets and transport interactions.
Entropy maximising spatial interaction or location model constrained at only one end of the trip, used by Wilson (1967) for shopping and residential choice contexts.
Time-series car ownership forecasting model using a logistic curve with saturation rate, developed by John Tanner at the UK Road Research Laboratory.
A modal split model applied to trip origins and destinations before distribution.
A modal split model applied to distributed zone-to-zone interchanges.
Generalisation of MNL introduced by McFadden as a basis for statistical exploration of IIA failures.
Probabilistic binary mode choice model developed by Stanley Warner using discriminant and regression analysis on Chicago commuter data.
Car ownership forecasting model developed by Wootton and Pick using conditional probabilities of car ownership against household income, applied in the West Midlands Study.
Contractor for the Urban Mass Transit Planning Project.
Chicago research institute contracted to investigate shortest-route computation for CATS.
U.S. federal road agency that developed travel forecasting methods, manuals and computer programs.
Dutch organization that conducted a 1973-74 study with Cambridge Systematics applying disaggregate probabilistic travel demand models in the Netherlands.
Consultancy associated with transfer of disaggregate travel demand modelling ideas to the Netherlands.
Organization associated in the source with a pioneering 1972 study on model structures within a discrete choice framework.
Consultants who undertook a 1972-73 Channel Tunnel economic assessment including a hierarchical freight model.
Computer bureau (later BARIC) used by the Worcester Transport Study in the mid 1960s for batch processing of model runs.
US agency for which Charles River Associates conducted the 1972 study described in section 4.4.

canonical_name: Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith and Associates

names.text: Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith and Associates

summary_en: Consulting consortium that carried out the London Traffic Survey and developed the Transport Analysis Programs (TAP) in the UK in the 1960s.

Consultancy that evolved from Cambridge Systematics Europe and carried out disaggregate travel demand studies in the Netherlands, including the South Wing study, transferability research and the Dutch national model.

canonical_name: Institute for Transport Studies at the University of Leeds

names.text: Institute for Transport Studies at the University of Leeds

summary_en: University of Leeds research institute where Huw Williams and Martyn Senior developed nested logit formulations and applied them to the Leeds–West Yorkshire study area.
UK research unit that applied disaggregate mode-choice methods to journey-to-work studies in the late 1960s and early 1970s.
Unit established by UK minister of transport Ernest Marples to manage London traffic in the late 1950s.
Unit within the Economic Planning Directorate of the UK Ministry of Transport where Alan Wilson led work on transport evaluation methodology.
San Francisco Bay Area planning organization implementing individual choice models in the mid-1970s according to the source.
Consultants who undertook an early individual choice model application for the San Diego County Planning Organization.
Planning agency for which Peat, Marwick, Mitchell & Co. undertook an early individual choice model application.

canonical_name: Steering Group to the UK Minister of Transport

names.text: Steering Group to the UK Minister of Transport

summary_en: Group that reported to the UK minister of transport in the preface to the 1963 Traffic in Towns report.
Developer of the EMPIRIC model.
UK government department that took over transport responsibilities from the Ministry of Transport after 1970.
UK government ministry responsible for transport policy in the 1960s and sponsor of the first-generation urban transportation studies.
UK research organisation where John Tanner developed influential time-series car ownership forecasting models.
University where Daniel McFadden and colleagues undertook a prominent BART validation study.

canonical_name: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

names.text: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

summary_en: Federal department responsible for urban public transit planning during the period described.
US federal department associated in the source with the Spear 1977 report and the Urban Transportation Planning System.
Co-author with John Tanner of a 1974 TRRL paper on a general theory of travel demand.
British mathematical geographer and planner who led the Mathematical Advisory Unit in the UK Ministry of Transport and coined the term 'generalised cost'.
Economist and urban modeller whose 1982 text argued for the unification of urban economics, transportation planning and urban modelling using a random utility discrete choice framework.
Psychologist and behavioural scientist who proposed the elimination-by-aspects (EBA) model and, with Shmuel Sattath, the hierarchical elimination-by-aspects (HEBA) model.
Researcher cited by Boyce and Williams for drawing attention to early UK demand analysis and value-of-time work.
Economist whose 1987 text applied discrete choice econometrics to housing and urban systems.
Researcher whose 1965 graphical approach to the trade-off between time and cost differences influenced Quarmby's modal choice analysis.
Co-formulator of the Herbert–Stevens residential location model.
Researcher identified with the intellectual development of the Regional Growth Model.
Transportation researcher who, in 1974, compared the use of trip-end and trip-interchange modal split models in cities of different sizes.
Author of a 1977 US Department of Transportation report on early individual choice model applications.
UK researcher who developed a stochastic route choice method for assignment using rectangular link time distributions, applied in UK transportation studies from the late 1960s.
Transportation researcher who formulated the heteroscedastic extreme value (HEV) model and the maximum approximate composite marginal likelihood (MACML) approach.
British economist at the University of Oxford who led the team in which Alan Wilson worked and co-authored with Michael Beesley a 1963 cost-benefit analysis of the Victoria Line.
Researcher identified by Boyce and Williams with an early travel-choice analysis using random distributions of costs.
Senior UK civil servant who led the team assembled to produce the 1963 Traffic in Towns report.
US economist described by Boyce and Williams as an outstanding contributor to micro-level behavioural travel-demand modelling.
Co-author of the source book and co-author of a 1970 study analysing land use–transportation alternatives in the US.
Major contributor to the theory and application of stated preference and stated choice methods in transportation, often collaborating with Jordan Louviere.
British transport economist whose 1967 Leeds Ph.D. research on modal choice for the journey to work contributed to the value-of-time and generalised-cost methodology in UK transport evaluation.
British transportation analyst whose 1973 review of early UK transportation studies identified the unsuitable transfer of US trip-end modal split methods to the UK.
Author of Requiem for Large-Scale Models.
Mathematical psychologist associated in the source with axiomatic probabilistic choice models and the IIA axiom.
Mathematician who devised a label-correcting shortest-route algorithm.
Co-author with Moshe Ben-Akiva of the 1978 report on the Metropolitan Transportation Commission disaggregate travel forecasting model for the San Francisco Bay Area.

UK minister of transport in the late 1950s who formed the London Traffic Management Unit, initiated traffic studies in London and Glasgow, and asked Colin Buchanan to investigate car-related problems.
Researcher associated in the source with extensive numerical analysis and taxonomy of aggregation methods for discrete choice models.
Co-originator with John Wootton of the household-based category analysis approach for trip generation in the UK, at Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith.
Director of the first London Traffic Study, cited for a 1963 description of motor-age traffic problems.

Economist at the University of Minnesota whose work with collaborators in the 1950s and 1970s on evolving representative samples of individuals and households through Monte Carlo transitions is identified as an origin of large-scale dynamic microsimulation models.
British economist (1943–1998) who in 1971 analysed three methods for calculating user benefits from land use and transportation projects and linked economic evaluation to accessibility measures.

Co-author of the source book and researcher at the University of Leeds who in the mid-1970s developed tree-based nested logit formulations, parameter inequality conditions and logsum welfare measures for travel demand models.
Economist who formulated the Lowry Model.
Study director of DMATS and an early contributor to sequential urban travel forecasting.
Economist credited in the source with establishing probabilistic choice models based on random utility maximisation.
Researcher noted for an independent random-utility destination-choice study deriving logsum accessibility expressions.
Co-formulator of the Herbert–Stevens residential location model.
Developer of the passenger demand model mentioned in Chapter 4 note 25.
Co-author with Michiel Bliemer of reviews and explanations of experimental design strategies for stated choice experiments, particularly efficient designs for non-linear models.
UK Road Research Laboratory researcher who developed a widely used time-series logistic-curve model for car ownership forecasting in the 1960s.
British transportation engineer with US graduate training who joined Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith in 1962 and later developed the Transport Analysis Programs (TAP).
Pioneer of conjoint analysis and stated choice methods in transportation and market research, co-author of the standard text on stated choice methods.
Transportation economist whose work on scheduling choice, value of time, and value of reliability has been influential in travel demand modelling.
Economist and author of a widely cited text on simulation approaches to choice modelling, and co-developer of the mixed logit model.
Psychologist whose 1927 work is described as part of the older pedigree of distributed-utility choice models.
Transportation researcher who reviewed UK speed-flow and level-of-service functions for capacity restraint assignment in 1977.
University of Leeds colleague of Huw Williams who collaborated on nested logit model applications, logsum user benefit measures and the identification of the parameter size anomaly.

American transportation systems researcher (1937–2000) who in 1973 argued that the Urban Transportation Model System (UTMS) was no longer satisfactory and that a new generation of analysis tools was needed.
British planning scholar whose 1975 review contrasted UK and US approaches to land-use forecasting in transportation studies.
Transportation researcher who introduced the network representation for GEV models and studied the cross-nested logit model.
Co-author with John Rose of reviews and explanations of experimental design strategies for stated choice experiments, particularly efficient designs for non-linear models.
Contributor to CATS trip distribution and traffic assignment methods.
Researcher whose Ph.D. work is presented as an alternative approach to discrete-choice travel demand model structures.
Researcher at the University of Leeds who in the late 1970s applied microsimulation to model organised car sharing programmes in West Yorkshire.
MIT economist associated by McFadden with founding ideas for the CRA travel demand model.
Researcher who identified and applied the cross-nested logit (CNL) model within the generalised extreme value family.
Economist associated in the source with an influential 1960s random-parameter modal choice model.
Researcher associated with the Channel Tunnel freight model and a random-utility derivation of spatial interaction models.
Researcher who proposed the STOCH network loading method.
MIT economist associated by McFadden with founding ideas for the CRA travel demand model.
Planner who described the integrated approach adopted by PJTS.
Assistant director for research and planning in CATS.
Developer of the MASTER (Micro-Analytical Simulation of Transportation, Employment and Residence) urban microsimulation model in the early 1980s.
Co-author with Amos Tversky of the hierarchical elimination-by-aspects (HEBA) model, which structures alternatives in a preference tree.

Mathematician at the UK Local Government Operational Research Unit who, with Andrew Daly, developed a fully specified nested logit model and the Harris–Zachary conditions for translational invariant random utility models.
US transportation researcher whose 1962 Northwestern Ph.D. thesis pioneered the disaggregate, probabilistic modelling of binary mode choice.
Researcher credited in the source with the first joint disaggregate model for car ownership and work mode choice.
Researcher associated in the source with Daniel McFadden and the 1975 discussion of MNL and random utility models.
Practitioner at Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith and Associates attributed by Wootton (2004) with the origin of the Rule-of-a-Half user benefit measure.
Researcher credited with proposing the Channel Tunnel hierarchical freight modelling approach.
Researcher who proposed scaling the variance of unobserved effects in stated preference data to allow pooling with revealed preference data in discrete choice models.
PJTS analyst who outlined the Regional Growth Model.
The Michigan metropolitan area studied by DMATS.
Dutch region where Richards and Ben-Akiva's disaggregate urban travel model project was conducted.
U.S. legislation that designated the interstate highway system.
U.S. legislation that funded construction of the interstate highway system with user taxes.
U.S. legislation requiring a comprehensive urban transportation planning process.
Joint circular by UK ministers of transport and housing and local government in January 1964, endorsing the findings of Traffic in Towns and emphasising land use–transportation studies.
The Chicago survey whose recorded trips were analyzed with the Cartographatron.
The future expressway network plan prepared and iteratively revised by DMATS.

names.text: SIGMO study of the Amsterdam metropolitan area

summary_en: Dutch application of the MTC-style disaggregate modelling approach to the Amsterdam metropolitan area, undertaken by Cambridge Systematics in the late 1970s.

names.text: before-and-after study of the introduction of BART

summary_en: Berkeley study comparing pre-BART MNL forecasts with observed BART mode shares in 1975.
San Francisco Bay Area rapid transit system whose completion provided a validation setting for disaggregate RUM-based models.
The eastern Massachusetts planning project for which EMPIRIC was developed.
1972-73 economic assessment of the proposed Channel Tunnel linking Britain with France, including passenger and freight hierarchical models.
A metropolitan transportation study initiated in the mid-1950s that developed and applied major travel forecasting methods.
UK transportation study in the early 1970s that adopted a simultaneous distribution–modal split (G/D-M/A) structure.
A Detroit-area highway planning and urban travel forecasting study initiated in 1953.
National-level disaggregate travel demand model for the Netherlands, intended to produce forecasts of road and rail traffic flows for planning up to 2010.
Major Scottish traffic study initiated by UK minister of transport Ernest Marples in the late 1950s.
Conurbation land use–transportation study begun in 1964 and encouraged by the UK Ministry of Transport.
London transportation study in the early 1970s that adopted a simultaneous distribution–modal split (G/D-M/A) structure.
UK urban bus study in which Daly and Zachary applied a three-level nested logit model to mode–route choice among car, bus by multiple routes and walk.
The first London traffic study, directed by Gerard Drake, for which motor-age problems were described in 1963.
Third phase of the London Transportation Study, which applied cost–benefit evaluation using the Rule-of-a-Half as an approximation to consumer surplus.
Merseyside study begun in 1966 that adopted the Traffic Research Corporation trip-interchange modal split model.

canonical_name: National System of Interstate and Defense Highways

names.text: National System of Interstate and Defense Highways

summary_en: The interregional highway system designated in 1944 and funded for construction in 1956.
A Philadelphia-region study of integrated land use and transportation alternatives.

canonical_name: San Francisco Bay Area MTC Study

names.text: San Francisco Bay Area MTC Study
Metropolitan Transportation Commission San Francisco Bay Area study

summary_en: Landmark mid-1970s study for the San Francisco Bay Area Metropolitan Transportation Commission that integrated disaggregate models into a travel forecasting system and compared it with the traditional four-step approach.

names.text: South East Lancashire North East Cheshire Transportation Study

summary_en: Land use–transportation study for South East Lancashire and North East Cheshire begun in 1965 and encouraged by the UK Ministry of Transport.
First full-scale Dutch regional study based on disaggregate techniques, covering Rotterdam and The Hague and modelling travel by tours for eight purposes.
UK land use–transportation study begun in 1965, noted as an exception in its attempt to forecast land use systematically.
Tyneside land use–transportation study begun in 1967 and encouraged by the UK Ministry of Transport.
A HUD project to develop public-transport planning computer programs.
First major UK land use–transportation study begun in 1964, encouraged and co-funded by the UK Ministry of Transport.
West Yorkshire land use–transportation study begun in 1967 that adopted the Traffic Research Corporation trip-interchange modal split model.
Mid-1960s UK transport study that sent computer run specifications to English Electric Computers (later BARIC) via British Rail Red Star.
Control Data Corporation computer acquired by Wilbur Smith and Associates, prompting the development of the Transport Analysis Programs.
A first-generation mainframe used for CATS model computation.
Mainframe computer used in the UK in 1962 for analysing the London and Glasgow traffic survey data.
Economic choice theory in which individuals select alternatives according to utility maximisation with random components.
Software program developed by Andrew Daly for estimating nested logit and other discrete choice models.
A documented collection of BPR programs for highway travel forecasting.
A cathode-ray display device used by CATS to visualize recorded trips.
A program battery for modal split forecasting and transit network planning.
Berkeley procedure for generating synthetic representative household samples for urban areas, using Iterative Proportional Fitting and random sampling.
Bus transport system model developed by LGORU and applied in the Coventry Transportation Study before being extended to include mode–route choices.
Suite of programs developed by John Wootton at Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith for line-based public transport network representation and assignment in the UK.
Software written by Wootton and colleagues at Freeman, Fox and Wilbur Smith to incorporate innovative UK methods and models in the second half of the 1960s.
A 1970s transportation planning software system based in part on the HUD transit battery.
Work extending Ben-Akiva's Ph.D. findings to a joint MNL structure for shopping frequency, destination and mode.
Text by Alex Anas arguing for the unification of urban economics, transportation planning and urban modelling through a random utility discrete choice framework.
Cited work by Alex Anas on integrated land use and transportation modelling with a random utility discrete choice basis.
Cited work by Alex Anas on integrated land use and transportation modelling with a random utility discrete choice basis.
Cited work applying discrete choice theory to product diversity and consumer choice in economic markets.
Study cited by Quarmby (1967) that used a graphical approach to determine the trade-off between time differences and cost differences in modal choice.
Source cited by Boyce and Williams (2015) among the early 1970s critiques of conventional urban transportation model systems for policy insensitivity.
Work cited by Boyce and Williams together with Ben-Akiva 1973 on model structures within a discrete choice framework.
Study cited for car-pooling policy applications, micro-level household interdependency, sample enumeration, pivot-point MNL and before-and-after validation.
Paper by Moshe Ben-Akiva and Denis Bolduc on the mixed multinomial logit model in error components form.
Text by Moshe Ben-Akiva and Steven Lerman on discrete choice analysis and travel demand.
Study describing the SIGMO disaggregate travel demand model applied to the Amsterdam metropolitan area, based on the MTC approach.
Multi-author contribution to Marketing Letters on discrete choice methods with latent variables.
Multi-author contribution to Marketing Letters on discrete choice methods with latent variables.
Paper by Moshe Ben-Akiva and colleagues describing the logit kernel form of the mixed multinomial logit model.
Paper by Moshe Ben-Akiva and colleagues introducing the hybrid choice framework, integrating discrete choice methods with latent variable models.
Multi-author contribution to Marketing Letters on discrete choice methods with latent variables.
Multi-author contribution to Marketing Letters on discrete choice methods with latent variables.
Paper by Chandra Bhat formulating the heteroscedastic extreme value (HEV) model.
Paper by Chandra Bhat and Subodh Dubey on latent variables in discrete choice models.
Work by Michel Bierlaire introducing the network representation for generating GEV models.
Work by Michel Bierlaire studying the properties of the cross-nested logit model.
Paper by Denis Bolduc and Ricardo Alvarez-Daziano on latent variables in discrete choice models.
Cited text by Axel Borsch-Supan on discrete choice econometrics applied to housing and urban systems.
US study cited by Boyce and Williams for analysing land use–transportation alternatives.
Paper by Mark Bradley and Andrew Daly introducing the nested logit trick for accommodating differential variance between revealed and stated preference data within a single discrete choice model.
Review of UK land use–transportation studies cited by Boyce and Williams; contrasted UK land-use forecasting with US practice.
Source work in which Burrell developed a stochastic route choice method for trip assignment using rectangular link time distributions.
Cited work on the nested logit model by Juan Antonio Carrasco and Juan de Dios Ortúzar.
Pioneering study cited by Boyce and Williams for model structures derived within a discrete choice framework.
Study cited for diagnostic tests for failure of the IIA property in MNL modal choice applications.
Cited work formulating or reformulating an integrated land use and transportation model with a random utility discrete choice basis.
MIT study applying the Clark approximation to the multinomial probit model to simplify its numerical integration.
Cited work by Andrew Daly on the analytic and theoretical characteristics and practical implementation of the nested logit model.
Work by Andrew Daly proposing the recursive nested extreme value (RNEV) model.
Paper by Andrew Daly and Michel Bierlaire formulating the network-GEV model and showing its equivalence to the RNEV model.
Study describing the Dutch national model for forecasting traffic flows on major road and rail networks using disaggregate methods, artificial sampling and incremental (pivot-point) methods.
Study of the South Wing (Zuidvleugel) disaggregate regional travel model in the Netherlands, covering Rotterdam and The Hague, with explicit modelling of tours.
Cited work by Andrew Daly and Stan Zachary introducing a fully specified nested logit model, deriving Harris–Zachary conditions and applying it to the Huddersfield Urban Bus Study.
Cited work by Tomas de la Barra on integrated land use and transportation modelling.
Multi-author contribution to Marketing Letters on discrete choice methods with latent variables.
Source work by Robert Dial on public transport network assignment developed under US HUD contracts, cited in the context of UK and US independent developments.
Source work by Robert Dial that developed a practical stochastic route selection and trip assignment model in the US.
Source work by Robert Dial and Bunyan on public transport network assignment under US HUD contracts.
Revised publication of the CRA 1972 study cited for hierarchical discrete-choice demand modelling.
Work that interpreted the entropy function as a measure of dispersion or spread in travel patterns and derived equivalent trip distribution models by minimising overall travel costs.

canonical_name: Forecasting Urban Travel: Past, Present and Future

names.text: Forecasting Urban Travel: Past, Present and Future

summary_en: The 2015 source book by David Boyce and Huw Williams.
Paper by Mogens Fosgerau and Michel Bierlaire on the sensitivity of mixed logit results to the choice of mixing distribution.
Paper by Mogens Fosgerau, Daniel McFadden and Michel Bierlaire establishing necessary and sufficient conditions for additive random utility models using choice probability generating functions.
Seminal paper on cost-benefit analysis applied to the proposed Victoria Line extension of the London Underground, co-authored by Christopher Foster and Michael Beesley.

canonical_name: Freeman, Fox, Wilbur Smith and Associates 1966

names.text: Freeman, Fox, Wilbur Smith and Associates 1966

summary_en: Report on the London Traffic Survey (1961–62) cited by Boyce and Williams.
TRRL paper described as a historically important and technical contribution to random utility theory, consumer surplus and travel demand model construction.
Work cited for a critical practitioner view of newer disaggregate methods as excessively mathematical, theoretical and poorly packaged.
Unpublished Hague Consulting Group paper describing the Dutch transferability study using a scaling approach to transfer disaggregate models between regions.
Text by David Hensher and Eric Johnson on the econometrics of discrete choice applied to transportation.
Edited conference proceedings on discrete choice models by David Hensher and Peter Stopher.
The manual quoted for the historical definition of practical capacity.
Classic work by Harold Hotelling that introduced the generalised consumer surplus measure based on a path integral of demand with respect to cost, subject to integrability conditions.
Source cited for Bruce Hutchinson’s comparison of trip-end and trip-interchange modal split models in medium-sized and larger cities.
Work offering an extended discussion of the Rule-of-a-Half and its theoretical basis in transportation user benefit analysis.
Study developing the Harvard Urban Development Simulation (HUDS) microsimulation housing market model in the early 1980s.
Cited work by Frank Koppelman and Chandra Bhat on the nested logit model.
Cited work applying the random utility framework to the location of public services.
Work cited for a joint car ownership and work mode choice model.
Paper introducing the maximum simulated likelihood approach for estimating discrete choice models by replacing choice probabilities with simulation-based approximations.
Paper by Jordan Louviere and David Hensher on the stated choice formulation for SP data, using established discrete choice modelling tools.
Standard text on stated choice methods by Jordan Louviere, David Hensher and Joffre Swait.
Study by Roger Mackett presenting the MASTER (Micro-Analytical Simulation of Transportation, Employment and Residence) urban microsimulation model.
Comparative review of 45 UK land use–transportation studies completed before 1971, summarising the techniques applied and assessing the predictive accuracy of their forecasts against out-turn data.

Source cited for Marvin Manheim's critique that the Urban Transportation Model System (UTMS) was neither theoretically satisfactory nor relevant to the practical issues of the 1970s, and his call for a new generation of transportation analysis tools.
Cited work by Francisco Martinez on integrated land use and transportation modelling.
Cited work by Lars-Göran Mattsson on integrated land use and transportation modelling.
Work described by Boyce and Williams as setting out theoretical foundations, properties, estimation and early applications of MNL models based on RUM.
Daniel McFadden's 1978 paper introducing the generalised extreme value (GEV) family of discrete choice models, of which MNL and nested logit are special cases.

Daniel McFadden's 1981 chapter reviewing and synthesising discrete choice random utility models, comparing GEV, EBA and multinomial probit forms and developing additive income random utility maximisation (AIRUM) welfare analysis.
Daniel McFadden's paper adapting the generalised method of moments into the method of simulated moments for estimating discrete choice models.
Cited work by Daniel McFadden discussing the movement of early US researchers into other sectors such as energy, telecommunications, health care and environment.
Paper by Daniel McFadden and Paul Ruud further developing the method of simulated moments for discrete choice model estimation.
Paper by Daniel McFadden and Kenneth Train on mixed multinomial logit models and their approximation properties for random utility models.
Berkeley study describing the SYNSAM procedure for generating synthetic representative household samples using Iterative Proportional Fitting and random sampling.
Study by Toshiyuki Morikawa proposing variance scaling of stated preference data to allow pooling with revealed preference data in discrete choice models.
Paper by Toshiyuki Morikawa and Kuniaki Sasaki on latent variables in discrete choice models.

Final report of the UK value of time research programme undertaken by MVA Consultancy, the Institute for Transport Studies at the University of Leeds and the Transport Studies Unit at the University of Oxford, which gave official approval to stated preference methods in the UK.

Work by Henry Neuberger setting out three methods (I fixed demand, II Rule-of-a-Half, III Hotelling consumer surplus integral) for evaluating user benefits from transportation projects and linking them to accessibility.
Early US study that drew attention to household-based approaches for trip generation, cited as a precursor to Wootton and Pick's category analysis.
Cited work by Juan de Dios Ortúzar on nested logit applications in travel demand modelling.
Text by Juan de Dios Ortúzar and Luis Willumsen on modelling transport, referenced for discussions of stated preference, mixed logit and latent variables.
Work cited for a random-parameter modal choice model involving air and car alternatives between Californian cities.

names.text: Factors Affecting Modal Choice for the Journey to Work

summary_en: David Quarmby's 1967 Leeds Ph.D. study of modal choice for commuting to Leeds CBD, which derived behavioural values of time and informed UK generalised-cost practice.
Paper by Sebastián Raveau and colleagues on latent variables in discrete choice models.
Douglass Lee's 1973 critique of large-scale urban land use models.
Paper by David Revelt and Kenneth Train using the random parameters form of the mixed logit model to examine taste variation.
Book and study cited for an Eindhoven application of disaggregate and simultaneous probabilistic urban travel demand models.
Review by John Rose and Michiel Bliemer of experimental design strategies for stated choice experiments, including efficient designs for non-linear discrete choice models.
Report describing the Metropolitan Transportation Commission disaggregate travel forecasting model for the San Francisco Bay Area, including comparisons with the traditional four-step procedure.
Paper by Kenneth Small introducing the schedule delay concept in the context of commuting travel timing decisions.
Overview by Kenneth Small of what is known and not known about the value of time and the value of reliability.

Paper by Kenneth Small and Harvey Rosen that, alongside McFadden (1981), established theoretical foundations for using logsum measures and their extensions in welfare evaluation of projects and policies.
Work that offered an alternative 'minimum information adding' interpretation of the entropy maximising approach to trip distribution.
Cited work on the nested logit model in the context of practical compromise between generality and computational ease.
US DoT report cited by Boyce and Williams for early applications of individual choice models in urban travel forecasting.
Review of early UK transportation studies cited by Boyce and Williams; identified the unsuitable transfer of US trip-end modal split methods to the UK.
Work cited for views on the inconclusive evidence for simultaneous or recursive behavioural model structures.
Conference proceedings edited by Peter Stopher and Anthony Meyburg containing papers on discrete choice models.
Edited conference proceedings containing papers on discrete choice models.
Source work by John Tanner presenting the logistic-curve time-series approach to car ownership forecasting at the UK Road Research Laboratory.
1963 report to the UK Minister of Transport on the long-term consequences of car ownership and use for mobility and the urban environment, led by Colin Buchanan.
Cited work on the nested logit model in the context of practical compromise between generality and computational ease.
Cited text by Kenneth Train on discrete choice econometrics applied to transportation, housing and urban systems.
Text by Kenneth Train on simulation approaches to choice modelling, widely cited for its clarity and treatment of mixed logit models.
Amos Tversky's 1972 paper proposing the elimination-by-aspects (EBA) model as an alternative to the MNL model that avoids the IIA property.
Paper by Amos Tversky and Shmuel Sattath introducing the hierarchical elimination-by-aspects (HEBA) model, in which alternatives sharing aspects form a preference tree.
UK Department for Transport guidance or report referenced on mixed logit models.
Source cited for the finding that some heuristic capacity restraint assignment methods did not converge to the equilibrium solution.
Work by Peter Vovsha identifying and applying the cross-nested logit model to modal choice in Tel Aviv.
Source cited by Boyce and Williams (2015) among the early 1970s critiques of conventional urban transportation model systems for policy insensitivity.
Paper by Joan Walker on discrete choice models with latent variables.
Paper by Joan Walker on identification problems in mixed logit models.
Paper by Joan Walker and Moshe Ben-Akiva on latent variable models in discrete choice analysis.

names.text: Stochastic Choice of Mode in Urban Travel: A Study in Binary Choice

summary_en: Stanley Warner's 1962 Northwestern Ph.D. thesis on probabilistic binary mode choice between car and transit in Chicago.
Source cited for Marcus Wigan's review of UK speed-flow and level-of-service functions used in capacity restraint assignment.
Cited work by Huw Williams presenting tree-based nested logit formulations, parameter inequality conditions and logsum welfare measures.
Cited work by Huw Williams on nested logit and multinomial logit model structures, including multi-modal and mixed-mode tree structures.
Source cited by Boyce and Williams (2015) for the empirical comparison of Rule-of-a-Half and Hotelling consumer surplus estimates in transportation scheme evaluation.
Cited work by Huw Williams and Martyn Senior applying nested logit logsum measures to evaluate land use and transportation plans in West Yorkshire.

Alan Wilson's 1967 paper in Transportation Research that introduced the entropy maximising derivation of spatial interaction models, including the doubly-constrained journey-to-work model and the joint distribution modal split model.

names.text: Entropy in Urban and Regional Modelling

summary_en: Research monograph by Alan Wilson that assembled entropy maximising applications in travel forecasting, commodity flows, location, land use and urban systems theory.
Subsequent text by Alan Wilson that further developed the foundations of general urban models and the integration of land use and transportation models.
University of Leeds study using microsimulation based on synthetic spatial databases to examine micro-level interdependencies and distributional issues in heterogeneous populations.
Cited work by Alan Wilson and colleagues on integrated land use and transportation modelling and normative studies of land use.
Source cited for John Wootton's development of the TRANSITNET suite of programs for line-based public transport network representation.
Source cited by Boyce and Williams for John Wootton's recollections of early UK transportation studies and computing in the 1960s.
Source work in which John Wootton and Gerald Pick introduced household-based category analysis for trip generation in the UK.
Cited work by Stan Zachary deriving the conditions for probabilistic choice models to be underpinned by translational invariant utility functions.
